Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd set aside time to study, maybe 25 hours across the week, but I rarely stuck to any real schedule. I'd often find myself watching lecture videos late at night or flipping through materials whenever I felt like it. Being 28 and having other things going on in life, I just couldn't commit to the structured approach I probably should have had.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot, mostly driven by deadlines rather than any consistent weekly rhythm. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, then suddenly I'd cram when something was due. I tried to be more regular early on, but life kept getting in the way and I just drifted into this reactive mode of studying only when absolutely necessary.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline looming or getting a notification about something new posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything I was missing, which happened fairly often given my poor attendance record. The gamified tasks they introduced actually got me logging in more frequently for a while, though that motivation faded pretty quickly.
Q4: For a brief period, checking the LMS became somewhat routine, especially when the gamified elements were new and felt kind of engaging. I'd log in to see my progress on those tasks and check leaderboards. But it never really stuck as a genuine habit—it felt more like a temporary novelty that wore off, and then I was back to my sporadic engagement.
Q5: The video lectures were probably the most useful for me since I'm a visual learner. I watched quite a bit of video content, maybe close to 200 minutes total, because seeing concepts explained visually just clicks better for me. The discussion forums were also helpful when other students asked questions I hadn't thought of—saved me from having to ask myself.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the live sessions and anything that required real-time attendance—my 1% attendance rate speaks for itself. Reading materials that were just walls of text got skipped over too. I kept telling myself I'd get to them later, but later never really came unless it was directly tied to an assignment.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate relevance to an assignment or exam. The gamified tasks were interesting at first because they gave that quick feedback and sense of progress. But if something felt like busywork or wasn't clearly connected to my grade, I'd mentally check out and move on to something else.
Q8: Optional activities felt like suggestions I could safely ignore, honestly. Required work got done because it had to, but optional stuff? I'd tell myself it was extra credit I didn't need. There's something about the word 'optional' that my brain translates to 'skip' when I'm already feeling disconnected from the course.
Q9: Yeah, I did a lot of studying offline that wouldn't show up anywhere. I'd download lecture slides and videos, then review them on my tablet during commutes or when I didn't have internet. I also used external YouTube videos to understand concepts better—sometimes random educators explain things more clearly than the official materials.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was genuinely studying but the LMS wouldn't know. I'd print out materials and annotate them by hand, or I'd discuss concepts with a friend who was taking similar courses. Sometimes I'd spend hours working through problems in a notebook without ever logging into anything digital.
Q11: Absolutely. I'd download a video or PDF once, then never open it on the platform again. I preferred having materials saved locally so I could access them without dealing with slow loading times or needing to be online. The LMS probably thought I glanced at something once when I actually studied it multiple times offline.
Q12: There were definitely low points, especially mid-semester when everything felt pointless. I'd look at the workload and just feel this wave of sadness and disconnection wash over me. Being older than most students and feeling like I was just drifting through without clear purpose made it hard to care about logging in or participating.
Q13: Fatigue was a big factor—working and studying at 28 is different than doing it at 20. When I was tired or stressed about other responsibilities, the LMS was the first thing I'd neglect. I'd tell myself I'd catch up later, but the confusion about where I'd left off just made it easier to avoid altogether.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd have the LMS open, scrolling through materials, but my mind would be completely elsewhere. Just going through the motions without actually absorbing anything. Those sessions felt like a waste of time in retrospect, but I'd count them as 'studying' to make myself feel better.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts and peer interactions constantly, even though I knew they mattered. Something about putting my thoughts out there for others to see felt exhausting when I was already feeling low. I'd convince myself I'd do it later when I had something smarter to say, but that moment rarely came.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my only real motivator. I'd see something due in two days and suddenly become very productive. The LMS became much more active for me in those crunch periods. Without deadlines, I'd drift for weeks without meaningful engagement—they were the external pressure I apparently needed.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. Good feedback motivated me briefly, but critical feedback sometimes made me want to disengage more. When I got a decent grade, I'd think 'okay, I can coast a bit,' and when feedback was harsh, I'd feel too discouraged to immediately dive back in.
Q18: The course structure was pretty flexible, which sounds good but actually hurt someone like me who needs external structure. Without mandatory attendance or strict schedules, I just floated along doing the minimum. The gamified elements tried to add structure, but they felt artificial and didn't really change my underlying disconnection.
Q19: The visual elements and video content made engagement easier since that matches how I learn best. But the lack of consequences for missing live sessions made it too easy to skip them entirely. Having everything available asynchronously was convenient but also removed any urgency to stay on track.
Q20: I think that describes me somewhat—I was fairly active in the LMS, watching videos and checking things, but my actual output was inconsistent. Sometimes you're exploring, trying to understand, clicking around without producing anything concrete. Or maybe you're just procrastinating in a way that looks like engagement.
Q21: That definitely resonates with my experience. My exam score was decent despite barely attending anything live. I studied effectively offline and used my own methods that the LMS couldn't track. Performance and LMS activity aren't the same thing—you can learn without the platform knowing about it.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of access and time spent on the platform accurately. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if I was actually learning or just had a tab open while doing something else. They also miss all the offline studying that might be more valuable than clicking through slides.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling connected to the material and the course community. It's not just completing tasks or logging hours—it's having genuine curiosity and investment in your education. Honestly, I struggled with that feeling throughout this course, even when I was technically active.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show that I was going through a rough patch emotionally, feeling sad and disconnected most of the time. It wouldn't capture the internal struggle of being 28 and questioning whether I'm on the right path. All that context shapes how I engaged—or didn't engage—way more than any click data could reveal.
